Here is a common pattern at a big investment bank.=C2=A0Some nerds in the d=
erivatives lab cook up a derivative. To make it out of the lab and into pro=
duction, the derivative will generally have a few attractive characteristic=
s:
=09It comes with a good intuitive story. =E2=80=9CYou are worried about X, =
and this derivative will protect you from X.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9CYou expect t=
he market to do Y, and if the market does Y this derivative will make a lot=
 of money.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9CIt would be extremely surprising if the market=
 did Z, and this derivative will make money for you unless the market does =
Z.=E2=80=9D=09It has a pretty backtest. =E2=80=9CLook at this chart: This d=
erivative would never have lost money during your lifetime!=E2=80=9D (Or on=
ly rarely, or not very much, or only in conditions you don=E2=80=99t expect=
 to see again, etc.)=09It has a good name. =E2=80=9CWould you like to buy a=
 YES? That is the name of our product, YES, it is an acronym, would you lik=
e to buy it?=E2=80=9D The question suggests the answer, doesn=E2=80=99t it?=
=09It builds in a lot of edge, so the bank can make a lot of money selling =
it.

And then the nerds go out and tell the bank=E2=80=99s salespeople =E2=80=9C=
hey, we=E2=80=99ve got this product, it is called a YES, you should sell it=
 because it makes us a lot of money=E2=80=9D (Point 4). And they give the s=
ales force a memo that contains a summary of the product=E2=80=99s intuitiv=
e story (Point 1), and the pretty backtest (Point 2), and the name of cours=
e (Point 3), and also probably a longer explanation of how the product work=
s and what the risks are.=C2=A0And, ideally, the salespeople read the expla=
nation and look at the backtest and figure out the moving pieces and talk t=
o the derivatives nerds and get a good understanding of the product and sen=
sibly advise clients on how to use the product to achieve their particular =
goals.=C2=A0
But, in practice, some of the salespeople glance at the backtest and say =
=E2=80=9Caha! This is a product that can never lose money,=E2=80=9D and the=
y=C2=A0like the name,=C2=A0and they go out and tell their clients =E2=80=9C=
do you want a YES, it can never lose money,=E2=80=9D and the clients believ=
e them,=C2=A0and, uh:

The Securities and Exchange Commission [yesterday] announced that UBS Finan=
cial Services Inc. has agreed to pay approximately $25 million to settle fr=
aud charges relating to a complex investment strategy referred to as YES, o=
r Yield Enhancement Strategy.
According to the SEC=E2=80=99s order, UBS marketed and sold YES to approxim=
ately 600 investors through its platform of domestic financial advisors fro=
m February 2016 through February 2017. The order finds that, during this ti=
me, UBS did not provide its financial advisors with adequate training and o=
versight in the strategy, and although UBS recognized and documented the po=
ssibility of significant risk in YES investments, it failed to share this d=
ata with advisors or clients. As a result, the order finds, some of UBS=E2=
=80=99s advisors did not understand the risks and were unable to form a rea=
sonable belief that the advice they provided was in the best interest of th=
eir clients. When investors suffered losses, many of them, along with their=
 financial advisors, expressed surprise and closed their YES accounts.

The strategy is not=C2=A0that=C2=A0complex, but it is complex=C2=A0enough.=
=C2=A0It is an iron-condor-selling strategy: You sell some out-of-the-money=
 puts and calls on a stock index, and then buy back further-out-of-the-mone=
y puts and calls. The result if that the market doesn=E2=80=99t move much, =
you collect some premium; if the market moves, you have some losses, though=
 they are capped at around 10% to 20% of your position.
  [1]

This was cooked up by some nerds in the lab at=C2=A0Credit Suisse Group AG,=
 and worked well enough at Credit Suisse that UBS hired the whole lab in 20=
15, =E2=80=9Cpaying them upfront awards of approximately $50 million.=E2=80=
=9D Then they pitched it to UBS=E2=80=99s financial advisers, who went out =
and sold it to clients:
UBS opened YES to new clients in February 2016. The YES Team conducted road=
shows at UBS=E2=80=99s offices in California, Texas, and New York, among ot=
hers, and pitched the strategy over the phone to financial advisors located=
 across the country. The YES Team marketed the strategy as a way to enhance=
 returns on an existing portfolio of securities. The YES Team generally exp=
lained to financial advisors and clients that historically the strategy had=
 generated gains of approximately 3%-to-5% per year with worst-case histori=
cal losses of approximately 1% per year. The YES Team acknowledged that the=
 strategy had experienced losses up to 11% in a single month but explained =
that this created a potential profit opportunity because they could try=C2=
=A0to sell options at premium prices, sometimes analogizing the program to =
writing =E2=80=9Churricane insurance.=E2=80=9D As a result of these efforts=
, the number of client accounts increased by approximately 600 and the amou=
nt invested in YES increased by approximately $2 billion during the Relevan=
t Period.
But, says the SEC, the advisers didn=E2=80=99t really know what they were s=
elling:

Because there was no registration statement or other formal offering docume=
nt associated with YES, financial advisors and clients were dependent on wr=
itten materials prepared by UBS and documents associated with the account o=
pening process. The first was a blast email prepared by the YES Team highli=
ghting, among other things, that the strategy=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9Cworst year=
 was down 1.02%.=E2=80=9D The second was a 17-page slide deck. Beyond stand=
ard language along the lines of =E2=80=9C[s]ignificant market moves either =
up or down may result in losses=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9C[s]elling options inv=
olves a high degree of risk,=E2=80=9D none of the written materials provide=
d further explanation of the downside risk of YES. ...
Certain financial advisors and clients did not understand the significant d=
ownside risks of YES during the Relevant Period. Certain financial advisors=
 understood from communications with the YES Team that the strategy could e=
xperience significant losses but believed that this created a profit opport=
unity under a hurricane insurance analogy. Certain clients who invested in =
YES during the Relevant Period would not have invested in YES had they know=
n the significant downside risk, and believed their financial advisors woul=
d not have recommended YES had they appreciated those risks.

We have  talked about this product before; allegedly one broker told his cl=
ient that =E2=80=9Cif the world came to an end tomorrow, you=E2=80=99d be t=
he only one with any money left.=E2=80=9D From selling S&P iron condors! Co=
me on. Nobody in the derivatives lab would say anything like that. But some=
thing got lost in translation.
It=E2=80=99s hard. On the one hand, if you are a financial adviser, you wan=
t to give your clients good financial advice, which means understanding the=
 nuances of the products you are selling them. On the other hand, if you ar=
e a financial adviser at a big brokerage, you want to give them=C2=A0a lot=
=C2=A0of financial advice, both because you will endear yourself to (some)=

your employer=C2=A0by selling stuff that makes a lot of money for your empl=

=C2=A0understand the nuances of the product. If your understanding of a pro=
duct is limited to =E2=80=9Cthis thing is called YES and it never goes down=

      10b5-1s
   =20
 =20

One thing that I believe is that chief executive officers of public compani=
es know more about their companies than outsiders do. I think this is obvio=
usly true: The CEO spends all day working at the company, everyone at the c=
ompany reports to her, she sets the company=E2=80=99s strategy, she can get=
 all the information she wants about the company, etc.; she is just clearly=
 going to be more informed about the company than some random person, or ev=
en some hedge-fund analyst who covers the company closely.
But this=C2=A0is also a somewhat controversial thing to say in polite compa=
ny. There is in US securities law a sort of polite fiction that the CEO of =
a public company only sometimes has =E2=80=9Cmaterial nonpublic information=
=E2=80=9D about the company. Right before the company releases earnings, wh=
en the CEO has seen the draft earnings release but it isn=E2=80=99t public =
yet, she has MNPI, but midway through the quarter she does not. If she is a=
ctively negotiating a merger with a competitor, she has MNPI, but if she ju=
st has occasional hypothetical chats with the competitor=E2=80=99s CEO then=
 she does not. If the company just suffered a devastating hack that it hasn=
=E2=80=99t yet disclosed, she has MNPI, but if she is just reviewing weekly=
 cybersecurity threat assessments she does not. Etc. (Not legal advice!)=C2=
=A0
This polite fiction is important because it is what allows corporate execut=
ives to trade stock. If you said =E2=80=9Cwell, yes, obviously CEOs always =
have inside information about their companies, after all they run those com=
panies,=E2=80=9D then any time a CEO traded her company=E2=80=99s stock she=
 would be doing a crime. (It is illegal, in the US, for corporate insiders =
to trade while in possession of material nonpublic information.) So you say=
 =E2=80=9Coh no CEOs never have any material information about their compan=
y except at the end of the quarter, or when they=E2=80=99re doing a merger,=
 or when they get hacked.=E2=80=9D And then when things are normal =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0when the CEO just knows all sorts of inside information about her =
company, but none of it is big enough to rise to the level of =E2=80=9Cmate=
rial nonpublic information=E2=80=9D=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0then the CEO can tr=
ade.=C2=A0
The way that this typically works is somewhat convoluted. Instead of just d=
eciding to trade stock and then doing it, CEOs frequently set up Rule 10b5-=
1 trading plans. In a 10b5-1 plan, the CEO arranges with her broker to sell=
 some prearranged number of shares of stock over some prearranged period. (=
Or to=C2=A0buy=C2=A0stock, though executives tend to do more selling than b=
uying, since they are often paid largely in stock and need to turn it into =
money.) It could be =E2=80=9C1,000 shares a day starting next month,=E2=80=
=9D or some more complicated formula based on the price of the shares. But =
the idea is that you set up the Rule 10b5-1 plan in =E2=80=9Cnormal=E2=80=
=9D times =E2=80=94=C2=A0when the polite fiction says that you don=E2=80=99=
t have any MNPI =E2=80=94=C2=A0and then the plan operates automatically. So=
 if you later=C2=A0get=C2=A0MNPI (because the quarter ends, or you start ne=
gotiating a merger), your broker keeps automatically selling stock for you,=
 and you can say =E2=80=9Cwhat, I=E2=80=99m not insider trading, this is ju=
st the plan operating automatically.=E2=80=9D
But the polite fiction isn=E2=80=99t=C2=A0true, so you can always get artic=
les like this:

A Wall Street Journal analysis of 75,000 prearranged stock sales by corpora=
te insiders, using a comprehensive compilation of the data, shows that abou=
t a fifth of them occurred within 60 trading days of a plan=E2=80=99s adopt=
ion. The timing in aggregate made the trades more profitable: On average, t=
hose trades preceded a downturn in share price more often than when insider=
s waited longer to trade, the analysis found.
Collectively, insiders who sold within 60 days reaped $500 million more in =
profits than they would have if they sold three months later, according to =
the analysis, which examined trades from 2016 through 2021 and adjusted ret=
urns to remove the effect of sector-wide moves in the market.
For those who waited 120 days or more after adopting a plan, roughly half s=
old before a downturn and half before a stock upturn, suggesting that the s=
ellers reaped no unusual gains after more time had passed.

One way to read this is that corporate executives know more about what will=
 happen at their companies in the next 60 days than the market does, though=
 their informational advantage erodes after 120 days. Really it is a bit mo=
re complicated than that. For one thing, executives probably sell stock mor=
e to buy houses or pay tuition than to make tactical bets on market prices,=
 so you might=C2=A0not=C2=A0expect their sales to be informative even if th=
ey do know more than everyone else. Some research has found that insider=C2=
=A0buying=C2=A0is informative (CEOs buy their own stock because they think,=
 with reason, that it will go up), but insider=C2=A0selling=C2=A0is not inf=
ormative (CEOs sell their own stock for liquidity reasons, not because they=
 think it will go down). Still, if you are a corporate executive and you de=
cide you need to sell stock this month, that does seem to increase the like=
lihood that next month will be bad.
Anyway there is an obvious solution to this problem, if it=E2=80=99s a prob=
lem. You can abandon the polite fiction that CEOs don=E2=80=99t have materi=
al nonpublic information, and replace it with a time delay. A CEO who decid=
es to sell stock today, or next week, knows something that the market doesn=
=E2=80=99t. A CEO who decides today =E2=80=9CI am going to sell stock in fo=
ur months=E2=80=9D does not. The stock price might be higher or lower in fo=
ur months; she doesn=E2=80=99t know. After she sells, the stock might go up=
 or down; she doesn=E2=80=99t know that either.=C2=A0In four months=C2=A0sh=
e=E2=80=99ll know stuff that the market doesn=E2=80=99t, but if she makes t=
he decision today it is probably an uninformed decision. That is both intui=
tive, and what the Journal=E2=80=99s analysis shows.
Also this is exactly what the US Securities and Exchange Commission wants t=
o do. Late last year, the SEC proposed amendments to Rule 10b5-1 to =E2=80=
=9Crequire a Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement entered into by officers or di=
rectors to include a 120-day mandatory cooling-off period before any tradin=
g can commence under the trading arrangement after its adoption.=E2=80=9D (=
We  talked about the proposal=C2=A0in December.) You sign up a plan today, =
but it can=E2=80=99t start trading for four months. You know stuff now that=
 the market doesn=E2=80=99t know, but four months from now the market will =
know it, so you aren=E2=80=99t insider trading.=C2=A0

   =20
      Bailouts
   =20
 =20

One thing that sometimes happens in financial markets is that a firm runs i=
nto a liquidity crisis. It has borrowed a lot of money short-term to fund l=
ong-term investments, but its short-term lenders have gotten spooked and ha=
ve pulled their financing, and it can=E2=80=99t sell its long-term investme=
nts fast enough or at a high enough price to pay them back. When that happe=
ns, a classic=C2=A0solution is for some bigger and better-capitalized firm =
with an appropriately long-term horizon to buy the troubled firm or its ass=
ets. =E2=80=9CWe know that these assets are good,=E2=80=9D the bigger firm =
might think, =E2=80=9Cso we will buy them at a discount, solve the liquidit=
y crisis and get rich.=E2=80=9D That is sort of the=C2=A0point=C2=A0of bein=
g a big and well-capitalized firm: You are a bit boring in the boom times, =
but then in the bust you can go around scooping up lots of good assets chea=
p.
But this is all pretty schematic, and in real life it is not always the cas=
e that a liquidity crisis is=C2=A0just, or=C2=A0primarily, a liquidity cris=
is. If some firm runs into a liquidity crisis and can=E2=80=99t pay back it=
s short-term debt and calls up a big well-capitalized firm for help, the bi=
g well-capitalized firm has to go look at its assets and see what=E2=80=99s=
 going on. Sometimes the big firm will crack open the books and conclude =
=E2=80=9Cyes, these assets are great,=C2=A0your lenders are spooked for no =
reason, it=E2=80=99s an amazing buying opportunity for us=E2=80=9D and buy =
them. Other times the big firm will crack open the books and find a crayon =
drawing of a billion-dollar bill and say =E2=80=9Cah, yes, that=E2=80=99s y=
our problem right there=E2=80=9D and walk away. Sometimes the liquidity cri=
sis is well deserved.
Anyway!

Crypto exchange operator FTX looked at making a deal with troubled crypto l=
ender Celsius but ultimately walked away, two people with knowledge of the =
matter told The Block.
FTX began talks with Celsius about providing financial support or making an=
 acquisition but decided against proceeding after looking at Celsius's fina=
nces, the sources said.=C2=A0Celsius had a $2 billion hole in its balance s=
heet and FTX found the company difficult to deal with, one of the sources s=
aid.
Celsius did not respond to The Block's request for comment.
Celsius has been fighting for survival since freezing all withdrawals on Ju=
ne 12, citing "extreme market conditions." Clients' funds have remained stu=
ck ever since.

See if I was looking for a bailout I would want to be=C2=A0easy=C2=A0to dea=
l with but that is just me.

      MicroStrategy
   =20
 =20

If you are MicroStrategy Inc., and you were a small software company, and t=
hen you pivoted to become a giant leveraged=C2=A0sack of Bitcoins with a sm=
all software company hanging off of it, and then the price of Bitcoin went =
way down, what are you going to do about it? Like if you run a hedge fund, =
or Tesla Inc., and you buy some Bitcoins and the trade moves against you, y=
ou can say =E2=80=9Coops, our bad, sorry we burned some of your money, movi=
ng on.=E2=80=9D But if you run a company that was a regular company but tha=
t has now=C2=A0turned Bitcoin into its whole identity, that is a trickier m=
ove. Your shareholders are all Bitcoiners, your debts are all secured by Bi=
tcoin, you are a Bitcoin company now, and there is no face-saving way out o=
f the trade.=C2=A0This also applies to El Salvador.=C2=A0Anyway:
On June 29, 2022, MicroStrategy Incorporated (=E2=80=9CMicroStrategy=E2=80=
=9D) announced that, during the period between May 3, 2022 and June 28, 202=
2, MicroStrategy acquired approximately 480 bitcoins for approximately $10.=
0 million in cash, at an average price of approximately $20,817 per bitcoin=
, inclusive of fees and expenses. As of June 28, 2022, MicroStrategy, toget=
her with its subsidiaries, held an aggregate of approximately 129,699 bitco=
ins, which were acquired at an aggregate purchase price of approximately $3=
.98 billion and an average purchase price of approximately $30,664 per bitc=
oin, inclusive of fees and expenses.
The good news is that, if it keeps going down and they keep buying, that av=
erage price will keep getting lower.

   =20
      Ethics
   =20
 =20

We  talked the other day about how, to become a=C2=A0certified public accou=
ntant, you=C2=A0have to take an ethics exam. You take this take-home open-b=
ook online multiple-choice ethics exam after passing four other grueling ex=
ams about accounting, and it seems to be taken less seriously than some of =
the other elements of becoming an accountant. To the extent that a bunch of=
 accountants at Ernst & Young cheated on it, and the US Securities and Exch=
ange Commission caught them and fined E&Y=C2=A0$100 million for not turning=
 in the cheaters.
I suggested that a regulatorily imposed multiple-choice ethics exam is mayb=
e not the best way to instill a sense of ethics in a profession.
A reader pointed me to this paper, by Margaret Forster, Tim Loughran and Bi=
ll McDonald at Notre Dame, about =E2=80=9CCommonality in Codes of Ethics.=
=E2=80=9D From the abstract:
We create a database of company codes of ethics from firms listed on the St=
andard & Poor=E2=80=99s 500 Index and, separately, a sample of small firms.=
 The SEC believes that =E2=80=9Cethics codes do, and should, vary from comp=
any to company.=E2=80=9D Using textual analysis techniques, we measure the =
extent of commonality across the documents. We find substantial levels of c=
ommon sentences used by the firms, including a few cases where the codes of=
 ethics are essentially identical. We consider these results in the context=
 of legal statements versus value statements. While legal writing often man=
dates duplication, we argue that value-based statements should be held to a=
 higher standard of originality. Our evidence is consistent with isomorphic=
 pressures on smaller firms to conform.
The examples are fun; popular sentences include =E2=80=9CTheft, carelessnes=
s, and waste have a direct impact on the company=E2=80=99s profitability,=
=E2=80=9D=C2=A0=E2=80=9CWe seek competitive advantages through superior per=
formance, never through unethical or illegal business practices,=E2=80=9D =
=E2=80=9CRemember that it is your supervisor=E2=80=99s responsibility to he=
lp solve problems,=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9CStealing proprietary information, poss=
essing trade secret information that was obtained without the owner=E2=80=
=99s consent, or inducing such disclosures by past or present employees of =
other companies is prohibited,=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9CIt may help to get oth=
ers involved and discuss the problem.=E2=80=9D
It turns out that when the US Securities and Exchange Commission=C2=A0manda=
ted a code of ethics for public companies, they =E2=80=A6=C2=A0copied each =
others=E2=80=99 answers. Just like the accountants did! I feel like there i=
s a deep lesson here about ethics mandates.=C2=A0

10px;">
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><strong>Programming note:</strong><em> Money S=
tuff will be off tomorrow and Monday, back on Tuesday.</em></p>
<table class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" cellp=
adding=3D"0">
<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=
-table-lspace: 0pt; mso-table-rspace: 0pt;">
<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">YES!</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here is a common pattern at a big investment b=
ank.=C2=A0Some nerds in the derivatives lab cook up a derivative. To make i=
t out of the lab and into production, the derivative will generally have a =
few attractive characteristics:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">It comes with a good intuitive story.=
 =E2=80=9CYou are worried about X, and this derivative will protect you fro=
m X.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9CYou expect the market to do Y, and if the market doe=
s Y this derivative will make a lot of money.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9CIt would be=
 extremely surprising if the market did Z, and this derivative will make mo=
ney for you unless the market does Z.=E2=80=9D</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-b=
ottom: 5px;">It has a pretty backtest. =E2=80=9CLook at this chart: This de=
rivative would never have lost money during your lifetime!=E2=80=9D (Or onl=
y rarely, or not very much, or only in conditions you don=E2=80=99t expect =
to see again, etc.)</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">It has a good =
name. =E2=80=9CWould you like to buy a YES? That is the name of our product=
, YES, it is an acronym, would you like to buy it?=E2=80=9D The question su=
ggests the answer, doesn=E2=80=99t it?</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5=
px;">It builds in a lot of edge, so the bank can make a lot of money sellin=
g it.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And then the nerds go out and tell the bank=E2=
=80=99s salespeople =E2=80=9Chey, we=E2=80=99ve got this product, it is cal=
led a YES, you should sell it because it makes us a lot of money=E2=80=9D (=
Point 4). And they give the sales force a memo that contains a summary of t=
he product=E2=80=99s intuitive story (Point 1), and the pretty backtest (Po=
int 2), and the name of course (Point 3), and also probably a longer explan=
ation of how the product works and what the risks are.=C2=A0And, ideally, t=
he salespeople read the explanation and look at the backtest and figure out=
 the moving pieces and talk to the derivatives nerds and get a good underst=
anding of the product and sensibly advise clients on how to use the product=
 to achieve their particular goals.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But, in practice, some of the salespeople glan=
ce at the backtest and say =E2=80=9Caha! This is a product that can never l=
ose money,=E2=80=9D and they=C2=A0like the name,=C2=A0and they go out and t=
ell their clients =E2=80=9Cdo you want a YES, it can never lose money,=E2=
=80=9D and the clients believe them,=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloo=
mbergbusiness.com/click/28231054.277586/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9uZXdzL3B=
yZXNzLXJlbGVhc2UvMjAyMi0xMTc/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB17e5cd1e" itemprop=3D=
"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0000=
00; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bott=
om-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">and=
, uh</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The Securities and Exchange Commission [yester=
day] announced that UBS Financial Services Inc. has agreed to pay approxima=
tely $25 million to settle fraud charges relating to a complex investment s=
trategy referred to as YES, or Yield Enhancement Strategy.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">According to the SEC=E2=80=99s order, UBS mark=
eted and sold YES to approximately 600 investors through its platform of do=
mestic financial advisors from February 2016 through February 2017. The ord=
er finds that, during this time, UBS did not provide its financial advisors=
 with adequate training and oversight in the strategy, and although UBS rec=
ognized and documented the possibility of significant risk in YES investmen=
ts, it failed to share this data with advisors or clients. As a result, the=
 order finds, some of UBS=E2=80=99s advisors did not understand the risks a=
nd were unable to form a reasonable belief that the advice they provided wa=
s in the best interest of their clients. When investors suffered losses, ma=
ny of them, along with their financial advisors, expressed surprise and clo=
sed their YES accounts.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The strategy is not=C2=A0<em>that=C2=A0</em>co=
mplex, but it is complex=C2=A0<em>enough.=C2=A0</em>It is an iron-condor-se=
lling strategy: You sell some out-of-the-money puts and calls on a stock in=
dex, and then buy back further-out-of-the-money puts and calls. The result =
if that the market doesn=E2=80=99t move much, you collect some premium; if =
the market moves, you have some losses, though they are capped at around 10=
% to 20% of your position.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1" =
style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wi=
dth: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !importan=
t; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This was cooked up by some nerds in the lab at=
=C2=A0Credit Suisse Group AG, and worked well enough at Credit Suisse that =
UBS hired the whole lab in 2015, =E2=80=9Cpaying them upfront awards of app=
roximately $50 million.=E2=80=9D Then they pitched it to UBS=E2=80=99s fina=
ncial advisers, <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/28=
231054.277586/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9saXRpZ2F0aW9uL2FkbWluLzIwMjIvMzQtO=
TUxNjgucGRm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB07bb47b3" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" items=
cope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decorati=
on: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000=
; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">who went out and sol=
d it to clients</a>:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">UBS opened YES to new clients in F=
ebruary 2016. The YES Team conducted roadshows at UBS=E2=80=99s offices in =
California, Texas, and New York, among others, and pitched the strategy ove=
r the phone to financial advisors located across the country. The YES Team =
marketed the strategy as a way to enhance returns on an existing portfolio =
of securities. The YES Team generally explained to financial advisors and c=
lients that historically the strategy had generated gains of approximately =
3%-to-5% per year with worst-case historical losses of approximately 1% per=
 year. The YES Team acknowledged that the strategy had experienced losses u=
p to 11% in a single month but explained that this created a potential prof=
it opportunity because they could try=C2=A0to sell options at premium price=
s, sometimes analogizing the program to writing =E2=80=9Churricane insuranc=
e.=E2=80=9D As a result of these efforts, the number of client accounts inc=
reased by approximately 600 and the amount invested in YES increased by app=
roximately $2 billion during the Relevant Period.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But, says the SEC, the advisers didn=E2=80=99t=
 really know what they were selling:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Because there was no registration statement or=
 other formal offering document associated with YES, financial advisors and=
 clients were dependent on written materials prepared by UBS and documents =
associated with the account opening process. The first was a blast email pr=
epared by the YES Team highlighting, among other things, that the strategy=
=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9Cworst year was down 1.02%.=E2=80=9D The second was a 17=
-page slide deck. Beyond standard language along the lines of =E2=80=9C[s]i=
gnificant market moves either up or down may result in losses=E2=80=9D and =
=E2=80=9C[s]elling options involves a high degree of risk,=E2=80=9D none of=
 the written materials provided further explanation of the downside risk of=
 YES. ...</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Certain financial advisors and clients did not=
 understand the significant downside risks of YES during the Relevant Perio=
d. Certain financial advisors understood from communications with the YES T=
eam that the strategy could experience significant losses but believed that=
 this created a profit opportunity under a hurricane insurance analogy. Cer=
tain clients who invested in YES during the Relevant Period would not have =
invested in YES had they known the significant downside risk, and believed =
their financial advisors would not have recommended YES had they appreciate=
d those risks.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We have <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/28231054.277586/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGlu=
aW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMTktMDgtMjYvYmFua3MtY2FuLXQtZG8taXQtYWxsLWFueW1vcmU_Y21=
waWQ9QkJEMDYzMDIyX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3=
NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjIwNjMwJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b=
8524a319a23cB4680b371" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" targe=
t=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bor=
der-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: s=
olid; background-color: #ccc;"> talked about</a> this product before; alleg=
edly one broker <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/28=
231054.277586/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9hcnRpY2xlcy91YnMtZmFjZXMtY2xpZW50L=
WJhY2tsYXNoLW92ZXItb3B0aW9ucy1zdHJhdGVneS0xMTU2NjU1MjYwMA/607f07ceef4b8524a=
319a23cB9281f7f1" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"=
_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-b=
ottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid;=
 background-color: #ccc;">told his client</a> that =E2=80=9Cif the world ca=
me to an end tomorrow, you=E2=80=99d be the only one with any money left.=
=E2=80=9D From selling S&P iron condors! Come on. Nobody in the derivat=
ives lab would say anything like that. But something got lost in translatio=
n.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It=E2=80=99s hard. On the one hand, if you are=
 a financial adviser, you want to give your clients good financial advice, =
which means understanding the nuances of the products you are selling them.=
 On the other hand, if you are a financial adviser at a big brokerage, you =
want to give them=C2=A0<em>a lot=C2=A0</em>of financial advice, both becaus=
e you will endear yourself to (some)=C2=A0<em>clients=C2=A0</em>by selling =
them lots of whizz-bang stuff that they can=E2=80=99t get anywhere else, an=
d because you will endear yourself to=C2=A0<em>your employer=C2=A0</em>by s=
elling stuff that makes a lot of money for your employer. And doing=C2=A0<e=
m>that=C2=A0</em>tends to be easier if you=C2=A0<em>don=E2=80=99t=C2=A0</em=
>understand the nuances of the product. If your understanding of a product =
is limited to =E2=80=9Cthis thing is called YES and it never goes down,=E2=
=80=9D then you will be excited to sell it.</p>

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One thing that I believe is that chief executi=
ve officers of public companies know more about their companies than outsid=
ers do. I think this is obviously true: The CEO spends all day working at t=
he company, everyone at the company reports to her, she sets the company=E2=
=80=99s strategy, she can get all the information she wants about the compa=
ny, etc.; she is just clearly going to be more informed about the company t=
han some random person, or even some hedge-fund analyst who covers the comp=
any closely.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But this=C2=A0is also a somewhat controversial=
 thing to say in polite company. There is in US securities law a sort of po=
lite fiction that the CEO of a public company only sometimes has =E2=80=9Cm=
aterial nonpublic information=E2=80=9D about the company. Right before the =
company releases earnings, when the CEO has seen the draft earnings release=
 but it isn=E2=80=99t public yet, she has MNPI, but midway through the quar=
ter she does not. If she is actively negotiating a merger with a competitor=
, she has MNPI, but if she just has occasional hypothetical chats with the =
competitor=E2=80=99s CEO then she does not. If the company just suffered a =
devastating hack that it hasn=E2=80=99t yet disclosed, she has MNPI, but if=
 she is just reviewing weekly cybersecurity threat assessments she does not=
. Etc. (Not legal advice!)=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This polite fiction is important because it is=
 what allows corporate executives to trade stock. If you said =E2=80=9Cwell=
, yes, obviously CEOs always have inside information about their companies,=
 after all they run those companies,=E2=80=9D then any time a CEO traded he=
r company=E2=80=99s stock she would be doing a crime. (It is illegal, in th=
e US, for corporate insiders to trade while in possession of material nonpu=
blic information.) So you say =E2=80=9Coh no CEOs never have any material i=
nformation about their company except at the end of the quarter, or when th=
ey=E2=80=99re doing a merger, or when they get hacked.=E2=80=9D And then wh=
en things are normal =E2=80=94=C2=A0when the CEO just knows all sorts of in=
side information about her company, but none of it is big enough to rise to=
 the level of =E2=80=9Cmaterial nonpublic information=E2=80=9D=C2=A0=E2=80=
=94=C2=A0then the CEO can trade.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The way that this typically works is somewhat =
convoluted. Instead of just deciding to trade stock and then doing it, CEOs=
 frequently set up <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click=
/28231054.277586/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubGF3LmNvcm5lbGwuZWR1L2Nmci90ZXh0LzE3LzI0MC=
4xMGI1LTE/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBf21765d7" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemsco=
pe=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration=
: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; =
border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Rule 10b5-1 trading pl=
ans</a>. In a 10b5-1 plan, the CEO arranges with her broker to sell some pr=
earranged number of shares of stock over some prearranged period. (Or to=C2=
=A0<em>buy=C2=A0</em>stock, though executives tend to <a href=3D"https://li=
nk.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/28231054.277586/aHR0cHM6Ly9wYXBlcnMuc3N=
ybi5jb20vc29sMy9wYXBlcnMuY2ZtP2Fic3RyYWN0X2lkPTY4NzU4NA/607f07ceef4b8524a31=
9a23cBd02a49a5" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_b=
lank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bot=
tom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; b=
ackground-color: #ccc;">do more selling than buying</a>, since they are oft=
en paid largely in stock and need to turn it into money.) It could be =E2=
=80=9C1,000 shares a day starting next month,=E2=80=9D or some more complic=
ated formula based on the price of the shares. But the idea is that you set=
 up the Rule 10b5-1 plan in =E2=80=9Cnormal=E2=80=9D times =E2=80=94=C2=A0w=
hen the polite fiction says that you don=E2=80=99t have any MNPI =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0and then the plan operates automatically. So if you later=C2=A0<em>ge=
t=C2=A0</em>MNPI (because the quarter ends, or you start negotiating a merg=
er), your broker keeps automatically selling stock for you, and you can say=
 =E2=80=9Cwhat, I=E2=80=99m not insider trading, this is just the plan oper=
ating automatically.=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But the polite fiction isn=E2=80=99t=C2=A0<em>=
true</em>, so you can always get <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusi=
ness.com/click/28231054.277586/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9hcnRpY2xlcy9leGVj=
dXRpdmUtc3RvY2stc2FsZXMtcXVlc3Rpb25zLWluc2lkZXItdHJhZGluZy0xMTY1NjUxNDU1MT9=
tb2Q9aXRwX3dzaiZydT15YWhvbw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBf7ec2ea5" itemprop=3D"=
StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #00000=
0; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-botto=
m-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">arti=
cles like this</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A Wall Street Journal analysis of 75,000 prear=
ranged stock sales by corporate insiders, using a comprehensive compilation=
 of the data, shows that about a fifth of them occurred within 60 trading d=
ays of a plan=E2=80=99s adoption. The timing in aggregate made the trades m=
ore profitable: On average, those trades preceded a downturn in share price=
 more often than when insiders waited longer to trade, the analysis found.<=
/p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Collectively, insiders who sold within 60 days=
 reaped $500 million more in profits than they would have if they sold thre=
e months later, according to the analysis, which examined trades from 2016 =
through 2021 and adjusted returns to remove the effect of sector-wide moves=
 in the market.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">For those who waited 120 days or more after ad=
opting a plan, roughly half sold before a downturn and half before a stock =
upturn, suggesting that the sellers reaped no unusual gains after more time=
 had passed.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One way to read this is that corporate executi=
ves know more about what will happen at their companies in the next 60 days=
 than the market does, though their informational advantage erodes after 12=
0 days. Really it is a bit more complicated than that. For one thing, execu=
tives probably sell stock more to buy houses or pay tuition than to make ta=
ctical bets on market prices, so you might=C2=A0<em>not=C2=A0</em>expect th=
eir sales to be informative even if they do know more than everyone else. S=
ome <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/28231054.27758=
6/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuanN0b3Iub3JnL3N0YWJsZS8yNjk2NzU3/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23c=
Ba5a21423" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank"=
 style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-w=
idth: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgr=
ound-color: #ccc;">research has found</a> that insider=C2=A0<em>buying=C2=
=A0</em>is informative (CEOs buy their own stock because they think, with r=
eason, that it will go up), but insider=C2=A0<em>selling=C2=A0</em>is not i=
nformative (CEOs sell their own stock for liquidity reasons, not because th=
ey think it will go down). Still, if you are a corporate executive and you =
decide you need to sell stock this month, that does seem to increase the li=
kelihood that next month will be bad.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Anyway there is an obvious solution to this pr=
oblem, if it=E2=80=99s a problem. You can abandon the polite fiction that C=
EOs don=E2=80=99t have material nonpublic information, and replace it with =
a time delay. A CEO who decides to sell stock today, or next week, knows so=
mething that the market doesn=E2=80=99t. A CEO who decides today =E2=80=9CI=
 am going to sell stock in four months=E2=80=9D does not. The stock price m=
ight be higher or lower in four months; she doesn=E2=80=99t know. After she=
 sells, the stock might go up or down; she doesn=E2=80=99t know that either=
.=C2=A0<em>In four months=C2=A0</em>she=E2=80=99ll know stuff that the mark=
et doesn=E2=80=99t, but if she makes the decision today it is probably an u=
ninformed decision. That is both intuitive, and what the Journal=E2=80=99s =
analysis shows.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also this is exactly what the US Securities an=
d Exchange Commission wants to do. Late last year, the SEC <a href=3D"https=
://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/28231054.277586/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2=
VjLmdvdi9uZXdzL3ByZXNzLXJlbGVhc2UvMjAyMS0yNTY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB0255=
ba3d" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" styl=
e=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width:=
 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-=
color: #ccc;">proposed amendments</a> to Rule 10b5-1 to =E2=80=9Crequire a =
Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement entered into by officers or directors to in=
clude a 120-day mandatory cooling-off period before any trading can commenc=
e under the trading arrangement after its adoption.=E2=80=9D (We <a href=3D=
"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/28231054.277586/aHR0cHM6Ly93=
d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMTItMTYvdGhlLXNlYy1oYXM=
tYmlnLXBsYW5zP2NtcGlkPUJCRDA2MzAyMl9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdX=
RtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIyMDYzMCZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzd=
HVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB2dc7db06" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> talked about the propos=
al=C2=A0in December</a>.) You sign up a plan today, but it can=E2=80=99t st=
art trading for four months. You know stuff now that the market doesn=E2=80=
=99t know, but four months from now the market will know it, so you aren=E2=
=80=99t insider trading.=C2=A0</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One thing that sometimes happens in financial =
markets is that a firm runs into a liquidity crisis. It has borrowed a lot =
of money short-term to fund long-term investments, but its short-term lende=
rs have gotten spooked and have pulled their financing, and it can=E2=80=99=
t sell its long-term investments fast enough or at a high enough price to p=
ay them back. When that happens, a classic=C2=A0solution is for some bigger=
 and better-capitalized firm with an appropriately long-term horizon to buy=
 the troubled firm or its assets. =E2=80=9CWe know that these assets are go=
od,=E2=80=9D the bigger firm might think, =E2=80=9Cso we will buy them at a=
 discount, solve the liquidity crisis and get rich.=E2=80=9D That is sort o=
f the=C2=A0<em>point=C2=A0</em>of being a big and well-capitalized firm: Yo=
u are a bit boring in the boom times, but then in the bust you can go aroun=
d scooping up lots of good assets cheap.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But this is all pretty schematic, and in real =
life it is not always the case that a liquidity crisis is=C2=A0<em>just</em=
>, or=C2=A0<em>primarily</em>, a liquidity crisis. If some firm runs into a=
 liquidity crisis and can=E2=80=99t pay back its short-term debt and calls =
up a big well-capitalized firm for help, the big well-capitalized firm has =
to go look at its assets and see what=E2=80=99s going on. Sometimes the big=
 firm will crack open the books and conclude =E2=80=9Cyes, these assets are=
 great,=C2=A0your lenders are spooked for no reason, it=E2=80=99s an amazin=
g buying opportunity for us=E2=80=9D and buy them. Other times the big firm=
 will crack open the books and find a crayon drawing of a billion-dollar bi=
ll and say =E2=80=9Cah, yes, that=E2=80=99s your problem right there=E2=80=
=9D and walk away. Sometimes the liquidity crisis is well deserved.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness=

=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;">Anyway</a>!</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Crypto exchange operator FTX looked at making =
a deal with troubled crypto lender Celsius but ultimately walked away, two =
people with knowledge of the matter told The Block.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">FTX began talks with Celsius about providing f=
inancial support or making an acquisition but decided against proceeding af=
ter looking at Celsius's finances, the sources said.=C2=A0Celsius had a $2 =
billion hole in its balance sheet and FTX found the company difficult to de=
al with, one of the sources said.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Celsius did not respond to The Block's request=
 for comment.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Celsius has been fighting for survival since f=
reezing all withdrawals on June 12, citing "extreme market conditions." Cli=
ents' funds have remained stuck ever since.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">See if I was looking for a bailout I would wan=
t to be=C2=A0<em>easy=C2=A0</em>to deal with but that is just me.</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Over 12 tweets on Monday, he decried =E2=80=9C=

 referring to the $17bn in redemptions it has processed in May and June.</p=
>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CEventually these hedge funds, that bo=

appen then?,=E2=80=9D he warned.</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I mean =E2=80=A6=C2=A0they=E2=80=99ll =E2=80=
=A6=C2=A0buy them back =E2=80=A6=C2=A0at $1? Like that=E2=80=99s the point?=
 Of Tether? What are we doing here? Like if you see a stock go from $10 to =
$100 and think =E2=80=9Chahaha this is a great short=E2=80=9D and short it =
at $100, one thing that might happen is that there might be a short squeeze=
 and the stock might go to $400 and stay there. If that happens, (1) you wi=
ll have to buy it back at $400 and lose tons of money, and (2) the people w=
ho own the stock will say things like =E2=80=9Cyes!=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Ch=
aha gotcha=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Cthis is exactly what we wanted.=E2=80=9D B=
ut if you see Tether trading at $0.9988 and you think =E2=80=9Chahaha this =

 it will say things like =E2=80=9Cwhat?=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Coh dear=E2=80=

a useful stablecoin if it trades very far from $1. If you are short Tether =

uy it back at $1.00. That costs you $0.0012!</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you are MicroStrategy Inc., and you were a =
small software company, and then you pivoted to become a giant leveraged=C2=
=A0sack of Bitcoins with a small software company hanging off of it, and th=
en the price of Bitcoin went way down, what are you going to do about it? L=
ike if you run a hedge fund, or Tesla Inc., and you buy some Bitcoins and t=
he trade moves against you, you can say =E2=80=9Coops, our bad, sorry we bu=
rned some of your money, moving on.=E2=80=9D But if you run a company that =
was a regular company but that has now=C2=A0turned Bitcoin into its whole i=
dentity, that is a trickier move. Your shareholders are all Bitcoiners, you=
r debts are all secured by Bitcoin, you are a Bitcoin company now, and ther=
e is no face-saving way out of the trade.=C2=A0This also applies to El Salv=
ador.=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/2823105=
4.277586/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9peD9kb2M9L0FyY2hpdmVzL2VkZ2FyL2RhdGEvMT=

nk" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-botto=
m-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; bac=
kground-color: #ccc;">Anyway</a>:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On June 29, 2022, MicroStrategy In=
corporated (=E2=80=9CMicroStrategy=E2=80=9D) announced that, during the per=
iod between May 3, 2022 and June 28, 2022, MicroStrategy acquired approxima=
tely 480 bitcoins for approximately $10.0 million in cash, at an average pr=
ice of approximately $20,817 per bitcoin, inclusive of fees and expenses. A=
s of June 28, 2022, MicroStrategy, together with its subsidiaries, held an =
aggregate of approximately 129,699 bitcoins, which were acquired at an aggr=
egate purchase price of approximately $3.98 billion and an average purchase=
 price of approximately $30,664 per bitcoin, inclusive of fees and expenses=
.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The good news is that, if it keeps going down =
and they keep buying, that average price will keep getting lower.</p>

2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDYtMjgvZG9uLXQtY2hlYXQtb24tdGhlLWV0aGljcy1leGFtP2NtcGlk=

=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;"> talked the other day</a> about how, to becom=
e a=C2=A0certified public accountant, you=C2=A0have to take an ethics exam.=
 You take this take-home open-book online multiple-choice ethics exam after=
 passing four other grueling exams about accounting, and it seems to be tak=
en less seriously than some of the other elements of becoming an accountant=
. To the extent that a bunch of accountants at Ernst & Young cheated on=
 it, and the US Securities and Exchange Commission caught them and fined E&=
amp;Y=C2=A0$100 million for not turning in the cheaters.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I suggested that a regulatorily imposed multip=
le-choice ethics exam is maybe not the best way to instill a sense of ethic=
s in a profession.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A reader pointed me to this paper, by Margaret=
 Forster, Tim Loughran and Bill McDonald at Notre Dame, about =E2=80=9C<a h=
ref=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/28231054.277586/aHR0cH=
M6Ly9wYXBlcnMuc3Nybi5jb20vc29sMy9wYXBlcnMuY2ZtP2Fic3RyYWN0X2lkPTEyNTE0ODI/6=
07f07ceef4b8524a319a23cC449e06a0" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"items=
cope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !imp=
ortant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bott=
om-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Commonality in Codes of Ethics</a=
>.=E2=80=9D From the abstract:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We create a database of company co=
des of ethics from firms listed on the Standard & Poor=E2=80=99s 500 In=
dex and, separately, a sample of small firms. The SEC believes that =E2=80=
=9Cethics codes do, and should, vary from company to company.=E2=80=9D Usin=
g textual analysis techniques, we measure the extent of commonality across =
the documents. We find substantial levels of common sentences used by the f=
irms, including a few cases where the codes of ethics are essentially ident=
ical. We consider these results in the context of legal statements versus v=
alue statements. While legal writing often mandates duplication, we argue t=
hat value-based statements should be held to a higher standard of originali=
ty. Our evidence is consistent with isomorphic pressures on smaller firms t=
o conform.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The examples are fun; popular sentences includ=
e =E2=80=9CTheft, carelessness, and waste have a direct impact on the compa=
ny=E2=80=99s profitability,=E2=80=9D=C2=A0=E2=80=9CWe seek competitive adva=
ntages through superior performance, never through unethical or illegal bus=
iness practices,=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9CRemember that it is your supervisor=E2=
=80=99s responsibility to help solve problems,=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9CStealing p=
roprietary information, possessing trade secret information that was obtain=
ed without the owner=E2=80=99s consent, or inducing such disclosures by pas=
t or present employees of other companies is prohibited,=E2=80=9D and =E2=
=80=9CIt may help to get others involved and discuss the problem.=E2=80=9D<=
/p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It turns out that when the US Securities and E=
xchange Commission=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/=
click/28231054.277586/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9ydWxlcy9maW5hbC8zMy04MTc3L=
mh0bQ/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB2e97a9cb" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">mandated a code of ethic=
s</a> for public companies, they =E2=80=A6=C2=A0copied each others=E2=80=99=
 answers. Just like the accountants did! I feel like there is a deep lesson=
 here about ethics mandates.=C2=A0</p>
